title: Listening - Part 3: Collecting and Collections
description: You will hear an interview with John Harvey and Meredith Jackson about their new book on collecting. Choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

content.audio_script:
Interviewer: Today I'm talking to John Harvey and Meredith Jackson, whose new book, 'The Art of Collecting', is out this week. John, let's start with you. What defines a collection? Is it simply about having a lot of something?

John: Well, it’s a common misconception. People think you need a room full of things, but [15]actually, they don’t have to be numerous at all.[/15] It's more about the intent and the relationship between the items. 

Meredith: I agree completely. [15]You might only have five items that are carefully chosen, or you could have hundreds[/15], but it's the cohesion that makes it a collection, not the sheer volume.

Interviewer: In your research, you talked to many collectors. Meredith, did you find they were particular about the look of their pieces?

Meredith: Oh, incredibly so. It’s not just about aesthetics in a general sense, but [16]collectors often set themselves these remarkably strict rules.[/16] I remember one woman who collected glass; she wouldn't even consider an item if it had a certain texture, even if it was the right colour and era. [16]She’d say, 'I wouldn't have that type in my collection!'[/16]. It's that rigid internal logic that I found fascinating.

Interviewer: John, you have a rather large collection of chairs. How did that start? Was it a lifelong passion?

John: Not at all. It was actually quite accidental. I was doing a module during my architecture degree and [17]I became fascinated with how chairs were made, and the forces that were put on them.[/17] I started looking at the engineering behind them—the principles of their structure—and I just had to buy one to see it up close. Then another, and well, here we are.

Interviewer: And Meredith, your collection of designer scarves started differently, didn't it?

Meredith: Yes, it was a treat, really. [18]I'd had a particularly full-on year at work, a real slog,[/18] and a client I’d been working for actually suggested it. [18]She said I should treat myself as a reward for everything I'd put in.[/18] I’d never been one for vanity or showing off, but I bought one, and the craftsmanship was so beautiful I was hooked.

Interviewer: John, back to the chairs. Your house must be full! Do you treat them as museum pieces?

John: Some people do, but I can't see the point. [19]I actually sit on mine.[/19] Visitors are often horrified, thinking they’re too precious, but [19]they were designed to be functional objects and I think they should be enjoyed as such[/19], not just kept in pristine condition for show.

Interviewer: Will you ever stop? Is there a point where you say 'enough'?

John: I tell myself that every time I run out of floor space! But the truth is, [20]something always pops up and tickles your fancy.[/20] You think you’re done, and then you see a rare piece at an auction. [20]I always seem to find room for one more[/20], so giving up isn't really an option that's on the table for me.

questions.question: John and Meredith share the opinion that, in general, collections
options: A) don’t often have much monetary worth.
B) reflect the life experiences of collectors.
C) aren’t defined in terms of quantity.
D) involve a considerable investment of time.
questions.explanation: Both speakers agree that a collection doesn't require a large number of items; it can be as few as five or as many as hundreds.

questions.question: When asked about collectors’ views on the appearance of the objects collected, Meredith highlights
options: A) the need to have distinctively-coloured items in a collection.
B) the strict rules that established collectors set themselves.
C) the willingness of collectors to share information with others.
D) the importance of how a collection is presented.
questions.explanation: Meredith notes that collectors follow 'remarkably strict rules' and rigid internal logic about what they will or will not include.

questions.question: What made John start to collect chairs?
options: A) a longstanding interest in comfort
B) a childhood instinct for the unusual
C) a wish to improve his academic reputation
D) a curiosity about the principles of their structure
questions.explanation: John's interest began with his fascination for how chairs are made and the 'forces' and 'engineering' involved in their structure.

questions.question: Why did Meredith buy her first designer scarf?
options: A) as a reaction to a colleague’s criticism
B) in the hope of making a profit
C) in a deliberate show of vanity
D) as a reward for doing a good job
questions.explanation: She bought it as a 'reward' after a 'full-on year' at work, following a client's suggestion to treat herself.

questions.question: John views the majority of the chairs in his collection as
options: A) functional objects to be enjoyed.
B) reminders of a golden age of furniture.
C) talking points to stimulate conversation.
D) precious pieces that need safeguarding from visitors.
questions.explanation: John emphasizes that he sits on his chairs because they are 'functional objects' and should be enjoyed rather than just preserved.

questions.question: John says that giving up collecting
options: A) would free up valuable space.
B) is unlikely to be an option for him.
C) would be a great loss to his life.
D) might actually be a sensible idea
questions.explanation: John admits that he always finds room for one more item that 'tickles his fancy', making it unlikely he will ever stop.
